explanation_md: `operator<<` prints each pair as `a,b`. Chaining works because the operator returns `out` by reference. So `x` prints `3,5`, a space is printed, then `y` prints `7,2`, then a newline.

prompt_md: Why must `operator<<` for a user-defined type be a free function rather than a member function?

options.a: Because `operator<<` cannot access private members when defined as a member function
options.b: Because as a member function the left operand would be the user-defined object, not `std::ostream`, which reverses the natural `cout << obj` syntax
options.c: Because `std::ostream` is a final class and cannot be used on the right side of a member function
options.d: Because member functions cannot return references

explanation_md: For a member function, the implicit `this` is the left operand. So `operator<<` as a member of `Foo` would require writing `foo << std::cout` — backwards from the intended syntax `std::cout << foo`. Free functions let you put `std::ostream` on the left.

options.a: The `friend` declaration is unnecessary — `operator<<` doesn't need access to private members
options.b: `operator<<` returns `void` instead of `std::ostream&`, so chaining fails because the result of `std::cout << a` is `void`, which cannot be the left operand of the next `<<`
options.c: The `out` parameter should be `const std::ostream&` to allow writing to const streams
options.d: Friend functions cannot have `void` return types

explanation_md: Chaining `std::cout << a << " and "` requires that `std::cout << a` returns something with a `<<` operator. Returning `void` means the result of the first `<<` is `void`, and you cannot call `void << " and "`. The fix: return `std::ostream&` and add `return out;`.

prompt_md: In `operator>>(std::istream& in, MyClass& obj)`, why is `obj` taken by non-const reference?

options.a: Because `std::istream` requires non-const objects as right-hand operands
options.b: Because the operator must fill `obj` with data read from the stream, so it must be able to modify it
options.c: Because `const` references cannot be passed to functions that take `std::istream&`
options.d: Because non-const references are faster than const references

explanation_md: The purpose of `operator>>` is to populate the object. If `obj` were passed by value, modifications would apply to a temporary copy that is discarded. If passed by `const` reference, you could not modify it at all. It must be a non-const reference so the changes persist in the caller's object.
`ss >> x` reads 7 into `x.m_v`. `std::cout << x` calls `operator<<` which prints `m_v * 2 = 14`. This also shows that I/O operators work on any stream, not just `std::cin`/`std::cout`.
